name: Linear Algebra Fundamentals
description: The core objects of linear algebra and what they mean geometrically: vectors, span and linear independence, basis and dimension, dot products, matrices as linear transformations, determinants, rank and null space, systems of equations, and eigenvalues and eigenvectors.

fields.front: In linear algebra, what is a vector in R^n?
fields.back: An ordered list of n real numbers.
fields.extra: Geometrically: an arrow from the origin to the point with those coordinates. Both views are used constantly.

fields.front: Geometrically, what does multiplying a vector by a scalar c > 1 do?
fields.back: It stretches the vector by a factor of c without changing its direction.
fields.extra: 0 < c < 1 shrinks it instead. This is why numbers are called *scalars*: they scale.

fields.front: Geometrically, what does multiplying a vector by a *negative* scalar do to its direction?
fields.back: It reverses the vector to point the opposite way.
fields.extra: The length still scales by |c|: multiplying by -2 flips the vector and doubles it.

fields.front: Geometrically, how do you add two vectors?
fields.back: Place them tip-to-tail; the sum runs from the first tail to the last tip.
fields.extra: Equivalent picture: the diagonal of the parallelogram the two vectors form. Componentwise it is just coordinate-by-coordinate addition.

fields.front: What is a linear combination of vectors v1, ..., vk?
fields.back: Any sum of scalar multiples of them: a1 v1 + a2 v2 + ... + ak vk.
fields.extra: Scaling and adding are the only two moves in linear algebra; a linear combination is anything you can build from both.

fields.front: What is the span of a set of vectors?
fields.back: The set of all their linear combinations.
fields.extra: Everything you can reach by scaling and adding them. One nonzero vector spans a line; two non-parallel vectors span a plane.

fields.front: Two non-parallel vectors sit in R^3. What does their span look like geometrically?
fields.back: A plane through the origin.
fields.extra: Not all of R^3: two vectors can never reach three independent directions.

fields.front: When is a set of vectors linearly independent?
fields.back: When no vector in the set is a linear combination of the others.
fields.extra: Every vector adds a genuinely new direction; nothing is redundant.

fields.text: Vectors v1, ..., vk are linearly independent exactly when a1 v1 + ... + ak vk = 0 forces {{c1::every coefficient ai to be 0}}.
fields.extra: If some nontrivial combination hits zero, one vector can be solved for in terms of the others: that is dependence.

fields.front: Geometrically, when are exactly two vectors linearly dependent?
fields.back: When they are parallel: one is a scalar multiple of the other.
fields.extra: With only two vectors this is the whole story; with three or more, dependence can hide in combinations without any pair being parallel.

fields.front: Why does including the zero vector make any set of vectors linearly dependent?
fields.back: Because 1 * 0 = 0 is already a nontrivial combination that equals zero.
fields.extra: The zero vector never adds a direction, so it can never be part of an independent set.

fields.front: What is a subspace of R^n?
fields.back: A subset closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication.
fields.extra: Closed means combinations never leave the set. Lines and planes through the origin are subspaces; a shifted line is not.

fields.front: Which specific vector must every subspace contain?
fields.back: The zero vector.
fields.extra: Multiply any member by the scalar 0 and closure forces 0 to be in the set. Quick test: a plane not through the origin is not a subspace.

fields.front: What is a basis of a vector space?
fields.back: A linearly independent set that spans the space.
fields.extra: Big enough to reach everything, small enough to have no redundancy. The standard basis of R^3 is e1, e2, e3.

fields.front: What does a basis give every vector in the space?
fields.back: A unique representation as a linear combination of the basis vectors.
fields.extra: Those coefficients are the vector's coordinates in that basis. Spanning gives existence; independence gives uniqueness.

fields.front: What is the dimension of a vector space?
fields.back: The number of vectors in any basis of it.
fields.extra: All bases of a space have the same size, which is what makes dimension well defined.

fields.front: A plane through the origin in R^3 is a subspace. What is its dimension? (type a number)
fields.extra: It takes exactly two independent vectors to span a plane. A line through the origin has dimension 1; R^3 itself has dimension 3.

fields.text: For u, v in R^n, the dot product u . v = {{c1::u1 v1 + u2 v2 + ... + un vn}}.
fields.extra: Multiply matching components, then add everything up.

fields.front: Is the dot product of two vectors a vector or a scalar?
fields.back: A scalar: a single number.
fields.extra: A common early mix-up. The *cross* product (in R^3 only) is the one that returns a vector.

fields.front: What does v . v equal, in terms of v's length?
fields.back: The squared length: v . v = ||v||^2.
fields.extra: Dotting a vector with itself sums the squares of its components, which is the Pythagorean theorem in n dimensions.

fields.text: The length (norm) of a vector: ||v|| = {{c1::sqrt(v . v)}}.
fields.extra: Equivalently sqrt(v1^2 + ... + vn^2).

fields.front: What is a unit vector?
fields.back: A vector with length 1.
fields.extra: Unit vectors carry pure direction; scaling one by c gives a vector of length |c| in that direction.

fields.front: How do you normalize a nonzero vector v?
fields.back: Divide it by its own length: v / ||v||.
fields.extra: The result is the unit vector pointing the same way as v.

fields.text: u . v = ||u|| ||v|| {{c1::cos(theta)}}, where theta is the angle between u and v.
fields.extra: This is the bridge between the algebraic formula and geometry: the dot product measures alignment.

fields.front: u and v are perpendicular (orthogonal). What number is u . v? (type it)
fields.extra: cos(90 degrees) = 0. This is *the* test for orthogonality in any dimension.

fields.front: What does a *positive* dot product tell you about the angle between two vectors?
fields.back: The angle is acute: the vectors point in broadly the same direction.
fields.extra: Negative means obtuse (broadly opposite); zero means perpendicular.

fields.front: A matrix is described as m x n. Which letter counts the rows? (type the letter)
fields.extra: Rows come first, columns second: a 3 x 2 matrix is 3 rows tall and 2 columns wide.

fields.front: What two operations must a transformation T preserve to be linear?
fields.back: Addition and scaling: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = c T(v).
fields.extra: Geometrically: grid lines stay parallel and evenly spaced, and the origin stays put.

fields.front: Where does every linear transformation send the origin?
fields.back: To the origin: T(0) = 0.
fields.extra: Set c = 0 in T(cv) = cT(v). A map that moves the origin (like a translation) is not linear.

fields.front: What do the columns of a transformation matrix A tell you?
fields.back: Where the standard basis vectors land: column i is A applied to e_i.
fields.extra: To write down a matrix, just record where e1, e2, ... go. This one idea decodes almost every matrix you meet.

fields.front: What does the product Av compute, in terms of A's columns?
fields.back: A linear combination of A's columns, weighted by the entries of v.
fields.extra: Av = v1 * (col 1) + v2 * (col 2) + ... This view makes column space and solvability obvious later.

fields.front: When is the matrix product AB defined?
fields.back: When A has as many columns as B has rows.
fields.extra: The inner dimensions must match: (m x n)(n x p) works and yields m x p.

fields.front: A is a 3 x 2 matrix and B is a 2 x 5 matrix. What is the shape of AB?
fields.extra: The matching inner 2s cancel; the outer numbers survive: (3x2)(2x5) gives 3x5.

fields.front: As transformations, what does the matrix product AB represent?
fields.back: The composition: apply B first, then A.
fields.extra: (AB)v = A(Bv), so read products right to left, like function composition f(g(x)).

fields.front: Which familiar property of number multiplication does matrix multiplication NOT have?
fields.back: Commutativity: in general AB is not equal to BA.
fields.extra: Rotate-then-stretch differs from stretch-then-rotate. Order of operations is real for matrices.

fields.front: What is the identity matrix I?
fields.back: The square matrix with 1s on the diagonal and 0s everywhere else.
fields.extra: Iv = v for every v: it is the do-nothing transformation, and AI = IA = A.

fields.front: What does taking the transpose A^T do to a matrix?
fields.back: Swaps rows with columns: the (i, j) entry moves to position (j, i).
fields.extra: An m x n matrix becomes n x m; the diagonal stays fixed.
The order reverses, same as with inverses.

fields.front: As a transformation, what does the inverse matrix A^-1 do?
fields.back: It undoes A: it sends Av back to v.
fields.extra: Algebraically A^-1 A = A A^-1 = I.

fields.front: Only matrices of what shape can be invertible?
fields.back: Square matrices (n x n).
fields.extra: A non-square map changes dimension going out or coming back, so it cannot be perfectly undone in both directions.
Socks and shoes: to undo 'B then A', undo A first, then B.

fields.front: What is a *singular* matrix?
fields.back: A square matrix that has no inverse.
fields.extra: Equivalently: det = 0, columns dependent, rank below n. 'Singular' and 'non-invertible' are synonyms.

fields.front: Geometrically, what does |det(A)| measure for a 2 x 2 matrix?
fields.back: The factor by which A scales areas.
fields.extra: In 3D it scales volumes. det = 3 means every region comes out 3 times larger.

fields.front: What does a *negative* determinant tell you about a transformation?
fields.back: It flips orientation, like a reflection.
fields.extra: In 2D, clockwise becomes counterclockwise; |det| still gives the area scaling.

fields.front: Geometrically, what does det(A) = 0 say about what A does to space?
fields.back: A collapses space into a lower dimension, squashing all area or volume to zero.
fields.extra: A 2D example: everything lands on a single line. Collapsed information cannot be recovered, which is why det 0 means no inverse.

fields.front: A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is what?
fields.back: Nonzero.
fields.extra: det(A) != 0 is the standard invertibility test; det(A) = 0 means singular.

fields.text: For a 2 x 2 matrix with rows (a, b) and (c, d): det = {{c1::ad - bc}}.
fields.extra: Main diagonal product minus anti-diagonal product.

fields.text: Determinant of a product: det(AB) = {{c1::det(A) det(B)}}.
fields.extra: Scale areas by det(B), then by det(A): scalings multiply.

fields.front: What is det(I)? (type a number)
fields.extra: The identity changes nothing, so it scales area and volume by exactly 1.

fields.front: det(A + B) = det(A) + det(B): is this a valid identity?
fields.back: No; the determinant is multiplicative over products, not additive over sums.
fields.extra: det(AB) = det(A) det(B) holds; nothing useful holds for det(A + B) in general.

fields.front: What is the column space of a matrix A?
fields.back: The span of A's columns.
fields.extra: Since Av is a combination of columns, the column space is exactly the set of all possible outputs Av.

fields.front: What is the null space of a matrix A?
fields.back: The set of all vectors x with Ax = 0.
fields.extra: Everything A crushes to the origin. Always a subspace, and always contains at least the zero vector.

fields.front: What is the rank of a matrix?
fields.back: The dimension of its column space.
fields.extra: Equivalently, the number of linearly independent columns; row rank always equals column rank.

fields.text: Rank-nullity theorem: for an m x n matrix A, rank(A) + dim(null space of A) = {{c1::n, the number of columns}}.
fields.extra: Every input dimension is either kept (rank) or crushed to zero (nullity); none go missing.

fields.front: An n x n matrix is invertible exactly when its rank equals what? (type it)
fields.extra: Full rank: the columns are independent and span all of R^n, so nothing is collapsed.

fields.front: When does the system Ax = b have at least one solution?
fields.back: Exactly when b lies in the column space of A.
fields.extra: Ax is always a combination of A's columns, so solvable targets are precisely the reachable ones.

fields.front: The homogeneous system Ax = 0 always has at least one solution. Which one?
fields.back: The trivial solution x = 0.
fields.extra: The interesting question is whether any *nonzero* solutions exist, i.e. whether the null space is bigger than {0}.

fields.front: Ax = b has two different solutions. How many solutions does it have in total?
fields.back: Infinitely many.
fields.extra: The difference of two solutions is a nonzero null-space vector, and adding any scalar multiple of it to a solution gives another solution.

fields.front: What is an eigenvector of a matrix A?
fields.back: A nonzero vector that A only scales: Av = lambda v.
fields.extra: It stays on its own line through the origin; the transformation does not knock it off its direction.

fields.front: What does an eigenvalue lambda tell you about its eigenvector?
fields.back: The factor by which A stretches or shrinks that eigenvector.
fields.extra: lambda = 2 doubles it; lambda = 0.5 halves it; lambda = 1 leaves it fixed.

fields.text: The eigenvalues of A are the solutions of det({{c1::A - lambda I}}) = 0.
fields.extra: Av = lambda v rearranges to (A - lambda I)v = 0, which has a nonzero solution only when A - lambda I is singular.

fields.front: Why is the zero vector never counted as an eigenvector?
fields.back: Because A0 = lambda 0 holds for every matrix and every lambda, so it carries no information.
fields.extra: Eigen*values* can be zero; eigen*vectors* cannot be the zero vector.

fields.front: What does having 0 as an eigenvalue tell you about a matrix A?
fields.back: A is singular: some nonzero vector gets sent to zero.
fields.extra: Av = 0v = 0 means the eigenvector lies in the null space, so det(A) = 0.

fields.front: What does a *negative* eigenvalue do to its eigenvector?
fields.back: It flips the eigenvector to the opposite direction while scaling it.
fields.extra: The vector still stays on the same line through the origin, which is why it still counts as an eigenvector.

fields.front: What makes a matrix symmetric?
fields.back: It equals its own transpose: A^T = A.
fields.extra: The entries mirror across the diagonal. Symmetric matrices have real eigenvalues and perpendicular eigenvectors.

fields.front: What does a diagonal matrix do to each standard basis vector?
fields.back: Scales it by the corresponding diagonal entry.
fields.extra: Diagonal matrices are pure axis-by-axis stretching, which is why diagonalizing a matrix makes it easy to work with.

fields.front: What is an orthogonal matrix Q?
fields.back: A square matrix whose columns are orthonormal, so Q^T Q = I.
fields.extra: Orthonormal: each column has length 1 and the columns are mutually perpendicular.

fields.front: What do orthogonal matrices preserve geometrically?
fields.back: Lengths and angles: they are rigid motions like rotations and reflections.
fields.extra: ||Qv|| = ||v|| for every v, and det(Q) is +1 (rotation) or -1 (reflection).

fields.front: What is the inverse of an orthogonal matrix Q?
fields.back: Its transpose: Q^-1 = Q^T.
fields.extra: This follows directly from Q^T Q = I, and it makes orthogonal matrices trivial to invert.
